perturbation.original_text: 1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information means any data or i n- formation that is propr ietary to or posses sed by a Party and not generally known to the public or that has not yet been revealed, whether in tangible or intangible form, whe n- ever and however disclosed, including, but not limited to: (i) any scientific or technical information, inv ention, design, process, procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method; The European IPR Helpdesk | P age 4 of 9 (ii) any concepts, samples, reports, data, know -how, works -in-progress, designs, drawings, photographs, development tools, specifications, software pro- grams , source code, object code, flow charts, and databases; (iii) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, oper a- tions, sales estimates, business plans and performance results relating to the Party’s past, present or future business activities, or those of its affiliates, subsidiaries and affiliated companies; (iv) trade secrets; plans for products or services, and customer or supplier lists; (v) any other information that should reasonably be recogni sed as Confidential Information by the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information means any data or i n- formation that is propr ietary to or posses sed by a Party and not generally known to the public or that has not yet been revealed, whether in tangible or intangible form, whe n- ever and however disclosed, including, but not limited to: (i) any scientific or technical information, inv ention, design, process, procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method; The European IPR Helpdesk | P age 4 of 9 (ii) any concepts, samples, reports, data, know -how, works -in-progress, designs, drawings, photographs, development tools, specifications, software pro- grams , source code, object code, flow charts, and databases; (iii) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, oper a- tions, sales estimates, business plans and performance results relating to the Party’s past, present or future business activities, or those of its affiliates, subsidiaries and affiliated companies; (iv) trade secrets; plans for products or services, and customer or supplier lists; (v) any other information that *may* reasonably be recogni sed as Confidential Information by the Parties.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'should reasonably be recognised' to 'may reasonably be recognised' introduces uncertainty. The original definition strongly implies that if information meets certain criteria, it *must* be treated as confidential. The modified definition weakens this, suggesting that even if information fits the criteria, it *might* not be considered confidential. This in-text contradiction appears in section 1.1 of the contract, it is contradicting on itself.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information means any data or i n- information that is propr ietary to or posses sed by a Party and not generally known to the public or that has not yet been revealed, whether in tangible or intangible form, whe n- ever and however disclosed, including, but not limited to: (i) any scientific or technical information, inv ention, design, process, procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method; The European IPR Helpdesk | P age 4 of 9 (ii) any concepts, samples, reports, data, know -how, works -in-progress, designs, drawings, photographs, development tools, specifications, software pro- grams , source code, object code, flow charts, and databases; (iii) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, oper a- tions, sales estimates, business plans and performance results relating to the Party’s past, present or future business activities, or those of its affiliates, subsidiaries and affiliated companies; (iv) trade secrets; plans for products or services, and customer or supplier lists; (v) any other information that *may* reasonably be recogni sed as Confidential Information by the Parties.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'should reasonably be recognized' to 'may reasonably be recognized' weakens the definition of Confidential Information. The original text implies a mandatory recognition based on certain criteria, whereas the modified text introduces discretion, potentially conflicting with the original intent. The original text uses language that strongly implies that certain information *must* be treated as confidential if it meets the listed criteria. By changing 'should' to 'may', the modification introduces an element of choice or discretion, which means that even information that fits the specified criteria might not be treated as confidential. This contradicts the original, more definitive language within the same section of the contract. The contradicted text is the Changed Text itself which has a conflict on what was defined and on the change made.

perturbation.original_text: 1.3 C onfidential Information shall be identifi ed either by marking it, in the case of wri t- ten materials, or, in the case of information that is disclosed orally or written materials that are not marked, by n otifying the other Party of the confidential nature of the info r- mation. Such notification shal l be done orally, by e -mail or written correspondence, or via other appropriate means of communication .
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3 C onfidential Information *may* be identifi ed either by marking it, in the case of wri t- ten materials, or, in the case of information that is disclosed orally or written materials that are not marked, by n otifying the other Party of the confidential nature of the info r- mation. Such notification shal l be done orally, by e -mail or written correspondence, or via other appropriate means of communication .
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'shall be identified' to 'may be identified' introduces uncertainty. The original text mandates the way confidential information is identified. The modified version makes it optional. This contradicts the overall intent of the agreement, which is to protect confidential information. This in-text contradiction appears in section 1.3 of the contract, it is contradicting on itself.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.3 C onfidential Information *may* be identifi ed either by marking it, in the case of wri t- ten materials, or, in the case of information that is disclosed orally or written materials that are not marked, by n otifying the other Party of the confidential nature of the info r- mation. Such notification shal l be done orally, by e -mail or written correspondence, or via other appropriate means of communication .
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'shall' with 'may,' making the identification of confidential information optional. This directly contradicts the purpose of protecting confidential information, creating an inconsistency within section 1.3 itself, as indicated by the provided explanation and the structural flaws definition. While both texts are identical, the change in wording creates uncertainty.

perturbation.original_text: 3.2 The Receiving Party will not disclose and will keep confidential the information r e- ceived, except to its employees, representatives or ag ents who need to have access to the Confidential Information for the purpose of carrying out their duties in connection with the permitted purposes specified in clause 2. The Receiving Party will inform them about the confidential quality of the informati on provided and will ensure that their agreement is obtained to keep it confidential on the same terms as set forth in this Agreement. Hence the Receiving Party w ill be responsible for ensuring that the oblig a- tions of confidentiality and non -use contained herein will be strictly observed and will assume full liability for the acts or omissions made for its personnel representatives or agents .
perturbation.changed_text: 3.2 The Receiving Party will not disclose and will keep confidential the information r e- ceived, except to its employees, representatives or ag ents who need to have access to the Confidential Information for the purpose of carrying out their duties in connection with the permitted purposes specified in clause 2. The Receiving Party will inform them about the confidential quality of the informati on provided and *may* ensure that their agreement is obtained to keep it confidential on the same terms as set forth in this Agreement. Hence the Receiving Party w ill be responsible for ensuring that the oblig a- tions of confidentiality and non -use contained herein will be strictly observed and will assume full liability for the acts or omissions made for its personnel representatives or agents .
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will ensure' to 'may ensure' significantly weakens the obligation of the Receiving Party to secure confidentiality agreements from its personnel. This creates a loophole where the Receiving Party could argue it took some steps but wasn't *required* to guarantee the agreement, thus reducing their liability for breaches by their personnel. The contradiction is within section 3.2: the first sentence states they 'will keep confidential' but then implies they might not ensure their employees do the same, which creates uncertainty about the level of protection provided and it is contradicting on itself.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.2 The Receiving Party will not disclose and will keep confidential the information r e- ceived, except to its employees, representatives or ag ents who need to have access to the Confidential Information for the purpose of carrying out their duties in connection with the permitted purposes specified in clause 2. The Receiving Party will inform them about the confidential quality of the informati on provided and *may* ensure that their agreement is obtained to keep it confidential on the same terms as set forth in this Agreement. Hence the Receiving Party w ill be responsible for ensuring that the oblig a- tions of confidentiality and non -use contained herein will be strictly observed and will assume full liability for the acts or omissions made for its personnel representatives or agents .
perturbation.justification: The change from 'will ensure' to 'may ensure' in clause 3.2 introduces a significant contradiction. The original text creates a clear obligation for the Receiving Party to ensure confidentiality agreements from its personnel. The changed text weakens this obligation, allowing the Receiving Party to potentially avoid full liability for breaches by its personnel. The contradictory text is 3.2 itself because while it dictates that the Receiving Party will keep confidential the information received, the Receiving Party now has an option to not ensure that their employees keep the confidential information. This creates an uncertainty about the confidentiality agreements.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1.1 This Agreeme nt shall remain in effect for a term of [number of months or years ]. Notwithstanding the foregoi ng, the Receiving Party’s duty to hold in confidence Conf i- dential Information that was disclosed during the term shal l remain in effect indefinitely, save otherwise agreed.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1.1 This Agreeme nt shall remain in effect for a term of [number of months or years ]. Notwithstanding the foregoi ng, the Receiving Party’s duty to hold in confidence Conf i- dential Information that was disclosed during the term *may* remain in effect indefinitely, save otherwise agreed.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall remain in effect indefinitely' to 'may remain in effect indefinitely,' the perturbation introduces uncertainty regarding the duration of confidentiality obligations. The original clause unambiguously extends these obligations beyond the agreement's term. However, the modification weakens the confidentiality obligations, making it questionable whether the Receiving Party is obliged to treat the information confidentially, and for how long, following the agreement's end. This creates in-text contradiction in section 4.1.1, it is contradicting on itself.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.1.1 This Agreeme nt shall remain in effect for a term of [number of months or years ]. Notwithstanding the foregoi ng, the Receiving Party’s duty to hold in confidence Conf i- dential Information that was disclosed during the term *may* remain in effect indefinitely, save otherwise agreed.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall remain in effect indefinitely' to 'may remain in effect indefinitely' introduces uncertainty regarding the confidentiality obligations. The original text ensures that the confidentiality obligations continue indefinitely. The changed text weakens the obligation, making it questionable whether the receiving party must treat the information confidentially after the agreement ends, which causes an in-text contradiction to the contradicted text, which is the same section.
